narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've built a solid academic foundation with an undergraduate GPA of 3.70—well above the typical 3.21 average—and an LSAT score of 37, slightly edging out the usual 36.70. Your family comes from an upper-middle income bracket, and you're planning to attend full-time, which is the norm for most students at 92.7%. However, the admissions model, which predicts the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 45.9%. This probability tipped the scales toward rejection, as the school uses these predictions to select applicants most likely to succeed on the bar and graduate strong. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, placing you in the bottom tenth of your peers that year—far below the average decile of about 5.5—which signaled potential challenges in sustaining performance over time. Counterbalancing that, your strong undergraduate GPA had the second-strongest positive influence, pushing the model toward expecting bar exam success by showing your capability in college-level work. Similarly, your LSAT score exerted a notable favorable pull, reinforcing that your test-taking skills align with those who pass the bar. Compared to typical students, your early-year decile of 1 (versus an average around 5.7) amplified concerns when combined with the later dip, even as your other metrics shone brighter than average. While no single element dooms an application, this pattern made the model wary of bar passage risk. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your later deciles, perhaps through post-baccalaureate options or targeted tutoring, and reapply with updated transcripts showing upward trends. Many students in similar situations, including Hispanic applicants, have succeeded by addressing these gaps head-on. Your strong GPA and LSAT give you a great foundation—use them to demonstrate resilience. Keep pursuing your legal ambitions; rejections like this often lead to even stronger paths ahead.
